text: Eight weeks of concurrent strength and endurance training does not improve running economy at marathon pace in recreational runners compared to endurance training alone.
Concurrent strength training (2 sessions per week, 120 min total) significantly increases maximal isometric knee extension force from 4.6 to 6.2 N/kg (p < 0.01) in recreational runners over 8 weeks.
Adding two strength sessions per week (120 min total) to marathon training does not increase body mass in recreational runners over 8 weeks.
Concurrent strength training focused on trunk (strength endurance) and legs (high intensity) does not alter stride length or stride frequency in recreational marathon runners over 8 weeks.

Replacing approximately 36 minutes per week of running with 120 minutes of strength training (240 vs 276 min running) does not impair submaximal blood lactate thresholds or VO2peak in recreational runners over 8 weeks.
specific_value: 240 min running + 120 min strength vs 276 min running

text: Eight weeks may be an insufficient intervention period for concurrent strength training to translate leg strength gains into running economy improvements in recreational marathon runners.
